column1: # Casbin policy — the data the model in casbin_model.conf operates on.
#   role     = one of admin | operator-admin | account-admin
#   resource = operator | account | user | scoped_key | cluster | export | api_user
#   action   = create | read | update | delete | sync
# A line means "role X is allowed action Y on resource Z" — nothing more.
# The COARSE check happens here; the FINE-GRAINED check (e.g. "operator-admin
# X is only allowed to touch operator-X's accounts
# in Go in PermissionService. If you remove a line here
# RPC is locked out for that role globally — useful as a kill-switch but be
# aware the Go check is the one that actually keeps tenants isolated.
# `#` lines and blank lines are ignored by the loader (see casbin_embed.go).
# admin — full access to everything. Reserve for break-glass /
# platform operators. Stored in api_users with role="admin".
# Scoped signing keys (per-account templates that delegate signing)
# Clusters (NATS resolver endpoints NIS pushes JWTs to)
# Export / import (full operator subtree dump or NSC archive ingest)
# API users (NIS-side accounts that log in to use this service)
# names which one). Can manage everything under that operator
# EXCEPT the operator itself (no rename/delete of their own org).
# Read-only on the parent operator (no mutate / no delete).
# Accounts: create + read + update; delete is admin-only.
# Users in any of their accounts: full lifecycle.
# Scoped signing keys in any of their accounts.
# Clusters: read + sync. No create/update/delete (cluster lifecycle is
# Export their own operator's subtree (read-only — no import).
# names which one). Mostly used by tenants/customers managing
# their own users. Read-only on the surrounding operator and
# Visibility into the parent operator and own account.
# Users within the account: create / read / update (no delete).
# Clusters: read-only (they need to know where to point clients).
